To sum it up, we can say that the cloud native world already provided alternatives before the sun setting of ingress nginx. We hope this small insight can help you in your journey of migrating away from ingress nginx.

】

CNCF 托管 Kubernetes 集群来运行一些用于内部目的的服务（即：codimd、GUAC、kcp）。

Kubernetes 项目宣布ingress-nginx 退役（不要与NGINX 或 NGINX Ingress Controller），这也会影响上述集群。因此我们开始寻找替代方案。

经过一番讨论，我们决定继续使用 gateway-api 并将其实现为 Envoy Gateway。

Envoy Gateway 是一个 CNCF 开源项目，用于将 Envoy Proxy 作为独立或基于 Kubernetes 的应用程序网关进行管理。网关 API 资源用于动态配置和配置托管 Envoy 代理。

网关 api 和 ingress-nginx 架构

ingress-nginx 可与一个 LoadBalancer 服务配合使用；入口控制器接收所有流量并根据入口对象配置进行分发。


另一方面，网关 API 是多层设计的：

基于此设计，可以为每个 HTTPRoute 和/或 TLSRoute 创建一个 Gateway 对象。 （每个Gateway在集群上创建一个LoadBalancer类型服务）

服务集群的配置

可以配置共享的Gateway对象并将其配置在多个HTTPRoutes上。这是与当前 ingress-nginx 部署最接近的配置，具有以下优点：

  • 成本和资源效率：单个网关意味着一个 LoadBalancer 服务，即一个云负载均衡器。多个网关 = 多个负载均衡器 = 成本显着提高。
  • 操作简单：管理一个网关比管理数十个网关更简单。我们有一个用于 TLS 配置、侦听器和整体网关策略的单点。
  • IP 地址管理：我们为入口点获取一个稳定的 IP。对于多个网关，我们需要管理多个 IP 和 DNS 条目。

此文件夹包含我们实现的所有设置：

  • 使用 Envoy 网关的 GatewayClass
  • 为 Guac、codimd 和 kcp 服务的共享网关。
  • EnvoyProxy 用于配置 HPA、服务类型和其他代理设置。
  • ReferenceGrants 允许网关跨命名空间访问 SSL 证书
    • 每个服务的
  • HTTPRoutes
  • BackendTLSPolict 用于处理后端 HTTPS 连接的现有 nginx 注释

    • 我们如何迁移

    我们有两个选择：

  • 使用另一个公共 IP 地址添加 Envoy 网关并配置 DNS 以在 ingress-nginx 和 Envoy 之间执行循环
  • 配置 Envoy Gateway 以使用当前 IP 地址并一次性转移全部流量。

    • 虽然第一个选项更安全，但为了操作简单，我们选择了第二个。

    保留的 IP 地址已作为 EnvoyProxy 配置的一部分推送到存储库：

    api版本：gateway.envoyproxy.io/v1alpha1
种类：EnvoyProxy
元数据：
  名称： ha-envoy-proxy
  命名空间：envoy-gateway
规格：
  提供者：
    类型：Kubernetes
    库伯内特：
      特使服务：
        外部流量策略：集群
        类型：负载均衡器
        补丁：
          类型：战略合并
          值：
            规格：
              loadBalancerIP: "146.235.214.235" # 云提供商保留的IP地址
              端口：
              - 名称：https-443
                端口：443
                目标端口：10443
                协议：TCP
                nodePort: 32050 # 用于外部 LB 后端和防火墙配置的固定 NodePort
...

    关键：外部流量策略设置

    我们最初遇到由于 externalTrafficPolicy: Local（默认）导致的连接失败。此设置导致 NodePort 仅侦听正在运行 Envoy Pod 的节点。当 Oracle 云负载均衡器对没有 Pod 的节点执行健康检查时，检查失败，将所有后端标记为不健康。

    证书怎么样？

    我们选择通过注释使用由 ingress-nginx 触发的现有证书：

    ---
api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类: 网关
...
规格：
  gatewayClassName: envoy
  听众：
  - 名称：https
    协议：HTTPS
    端口：443
    主机名：“*.cncf.io”
    :
      模式：终止
      证书参考：
      - 名称：guac-tls
        命名空间：guac
        种类：秘密
        组：“”
      - 名称：auth-dex-tls
        命名空间：auth
        种类：秘密
        组：“”
...

    但是，证书具有对 Ingress 对象 的所有者引用。这意味着删除 Ingress 会级联删除证书及其 Secret。

    在一行下面，从引用 Ingress 的所有证书中删除 OwnerReference：

    kubectl 获取证书 -A -o json | jq -r '.items[] | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.ownerReferences[]? | .kind == "Ingress") | "\(.metadata.namespace) \(.metadata.name)"' |读取 NS NAME 时 
做 
    kubectl 补丁证书 $NAME -n $NS --type=json \
      -p='[{"op": "删除", "path": "/metadata/ownerReferences"}]' 
完成

    跨命名空间证书访问

    由于证书存储在与网关不同的命名空间中，因此我们配置了 ReferenceGrant 资源以允许跨命名空间访问：

    api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1beta1
种类：参考补助金
元数据：
  名称：允许证书网关
  命名空间：codimd
规格：
  来自：
  - 组：gateway.networking.k8s.io
    种类: 网关
    命名空间：envoy-gateway
  至：
  - 组：“”
    种类：秘密
    名称：codimd-tls

    这种模式重复了 e每个包含证书的命名空间。

    HTTP 路由

    ingress2gateway 帮助从现有 Ingress 资源准备 HTTPRoute 对象。

    我们有一个带有后端 HTTPS 配置的入口的特殊情况：

    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol：HTTPS
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-ssl-名称：api.services.cncf.io
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-ssl-secret：kdp/kcp-ca
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-ssl-verify：“开启”

    为了使用 Envoy Gateway 实现相同的行为，我们创建了一个 BackendTLSPolicy：

    api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类：BackendTLSPolicy
元数据：
  名称：kdp-后端-tls
  命名空间：kdp
规格：
  目标参考：
  - 组：''
    种类： 服务
    名称：kcp-前端代理
  验证：
    ca证书参考：
    - 名称：kcp-ca
      组：''
      种类：秘密
    主机名：api.services.cncf.io


    疑难解答

    TLS 握手失败

    如果您在 TLS 握手期间遇到 SSL_ERROR_SYSCALL 错误：

  • 检查网关侦听器：确保在端口 443 上配置 HTTPS 侦听器
  • 验证证书已加载：检查所有引用的证书是否存在且可访问
  • 检查 ReferenceGrants：确保允许跨命名空间证书访问
  • 查看 Envoy 日志：
    • kubectl 日志 -n envoy-gateway-system -l gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=shared-gateway

    负载均衡器运行状况检查失败

    如果云负载均衡器显示后端不健康：

  • 验证 externalTrafficPolicy：应该是集群，而不是本地
  • 检查 NodePort 可访问性：从 NodePort 响应的节点进行测试
  • 检查运行状况检查配置：确保负载均衡运行状况检查与服务配置匹配
  • 检查防火墙规则：验证安全组/NSG 允许从 LB 子网到 NodePort 的流量

    • 未提供证书

    如果 OpenSSL 无法检索证书：

    回显 | openssl s_client -connect :443 -servername <主机名> 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text

    这表明证书未加载。检查：

  • 网关证书引用中引用了证书
  • ReferenceGrant 存在用于跨命名空间访问
  • 网关状态显示已编程：True

    • 第 2 天操作证书解析

    我们决定稍后迁移证书，以缩小迁移范围并轻松使用当时的当前证书。然而，当它们到期时，我们可能会遇到麻烦。您需要执行以下操作，以确保您的证书由 Gateway API + cert-manager 管理：

    1.确保 cert-manager 支持 Gateway API：

    您需要在 cert-manager 上启用网关 API 支持：

    api版本：argoproj.io/v1alpha1
种类： 应用
元数据：
  姓名： 证书经理
  命名空间：argocd
规格：
  项目：默认
  来源：
    仓库地址：https://charts.jetstack.io
    目标修订版：v1.17.2
    图表：证书管理器
    掌舵：
      价值观： |
        配置：
          enableGatewayAPI: true ## 确保它存在！

    2.更新ClusterIssuer：

    更新当前发行人或创建新发行人：

    api版本：cert-manager.io/v1
种类：集群发行者
元数据：
  名称： LetsEncrypt-Prod
规格：
  极致：
    首选链：“”
    私有密钥SecretRef：
      名称： LetsEncrypt-Prod
    服务器：https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    求解器：
    - http01：
        网关HTTP路由：
          家长参考：
          - 组：gateway.networking.k8s.io
            种类: 网关
            name: share-gateway ## 这是您的网关的名称
            命名空间： envoy-gateway ## 你的网关所在的位置

    3.为 cert-manager 注释网关

    您需要添加注释，就像我们为 ingress-nginx 所做的那样：

    api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类: 网关
元数据：
  名称：共享网关
  命名空间：envoy-gateway
  注释：
    # 需要与您在上一步中创建/更新的 ClusterIssuer 匹配
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: 让加密产品  
规格：
  gatewayClassName: envoy

    4.分开听众

    我们最初为所有主机设置了一个侦听器，但它们需要分开（除非您使用 DNS 解析器作为通配符证书）。

    api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类: 网关
元数据：
  名称：共享网关
  命名空间：envoy-gateway
  注释：
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: 让加密产品
规格：
  gatewayClassName: envoy
  地址：
   - 类型：IP地址
     值：146.235.214.235
  听众：
  - 名称：https-guac
    协议：HTTPS
    端口：443
    主机名：guac.cncf.io
    :
      模式：终止
      证书参考：
      - 名称：guac-tls-gw
        种类：秘密
        组：“”
    允许的路线：
      命名空间：
        来自：全部

    # 为 cert-manager HTTP01 求解器添加
  - 名称：http-guac
    协议：HTTP端口：80
    主机名：guac.cncf.io
    允许的路线：
      命名空间：
        来自：全部

  - 名称：http-api-guac
    协议：HTTP
    端口：80
    主机名：api.guac.cncf.io
    允许的路线：
      命名空间：
        来自：全部

    # 为 cert-manager HTTP01 求解器添加
  - 名称：https-notes
    协议：HTTPS
    端口：443
    主机名：notes.cncf.io
    :
      模式：终止
      证书参考：
      - 名称：codimd-tls
        种类：秘密
        组：“”
    允许的路线：
      命名空间：
        来自：全部

  - 名称：http-notes
    协议：HTTP
    端口：80
    主机名：notes.cncf.io
    允许的路线：
      命名空间：
        来自：全部
...

    5.删除多余的 ReferenceGrants

    由于新证书是在与 Envoy 网关（在我们的示例中为共享网关）相同的命名空间上创建的，因此我们不再需要 ReferenceGrants。我们删除了它们：

    kubectl 删除引用授权 --all -A

    结论

    从 ingress-nginx 迁移到 Envoy Gateway 需要仔细注意：

    • 证书所有权和跨命名空间访问
    • 云负载均衡器集成（NodePort、运行状况检查、externalTrafficPolicy）
    • 需要 HTTPS 上游连接的服务的后端 TLS 配置

    与 ingress-nginx 相比，Gateway API 的多层架构提供了更好的关注点分离，尽管它需要了解 ReferenceGrants 和 BackendTLSPolicy 等其他资源。

    综上所述，我们可以说，在 ingress nginx 落山之前，云原生世界就已经提供了替代方案。我们希望这个小小的见解能够帮助您完成从 ingress nginx 迁移的过程。

item.title: 【What I Learned at My First KubeCon + CloudNativeCon as a High School Speaker】作为一名高中演讲者，我在第一次 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 上学到了什么
item.description:
【

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 is one of the biggest open-source conferences in the world, organized by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (part of the Linux Foundation, which manages major projects like Linux). It brings together the global cloud native community, and being selected as a speaker is extremely competitive, with acceptance rates in the single digits.

This year, over 13,000 people gathered in Amsterdam in March. As one of the youngest presenters there, I got the opportunity to share my poster on hurricane prediction using open-source projects.

When I first arrived, I was honestly overwhelmed. The RAI convention center was massive, and stepping inside made it feel even bigger. It didn’t really hit me until then just how huge this event was.

The packed keynote room

My Poster Session

I originally built my hurricane model during a class in my junior year, using a modified version of Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). Over time, it evolved into a much bigger project.

I ended up combining tools like Kubernetes, kgateway, and vLLM to run different services. Along with FourCastNet and an improved version of my CFD model, the goal was to make hurricane prediction more efficient and affordable using inference-aware routing.

My poster was titled “Efficient Inference for Hurricane Data and Future Movement Prediction”. 

My poster

I wasn’t sure if anyone would even stop by. But pretty quickly, a crowd started forming, way more people than I expected.

I presented, which was a nervous but fun experience, and then demoed my project on my laptop and answered waves of questions. It was neat hearing from people why they came. People were working on similar weather models, curious what a High Schooler had to say, and one visitor was even from my hometown!

Presenting the poster to a group

Demo-ing my project on my laptop

Exploring sessions

When I wasn’t presenting, I tried to attend as many sessions as I could.

One of the coolest ones featured a high-altitude glider powered by cloud native technologies, showing footage of it flying over the Himalayas. It was awesome to see open source being applied in sustainable aviation with extremely low energy and emissions.

Another memorable session literally had a bicycle shop and a bike brought on stage. They used it to explain the CNCF project maturity lifecycle, which sounds abstract, but the analogy actually made it really easy to understand.

The Solution Showcases

I also spent a lot of time walking around the solution showcase.

There were tons of booths from companies and open-source projects. At the project pavilion, I talked to maintainers and contributors, which was honestly one of the most interesting parts. Hearing what motivates people to contribute to open source made me want to get more involved myself.

The scale of the event was kind of crazy: so many companies, all trying to stand out. A lot of booths had games, demos, or free swag to grab your attention.

I had to limit myself, though (luggage space is real), so I mostly stuck to the essentials: a conference T-shirt and the speaker gift, along with a few cute toys.

A funny thing that kept happening was people noticing my badge and going, “Oh! You’re  a speaker?!” Then I’d end up explaining my project all over again.

Also, I tried fresh stroopwafels for the first time (from a booth at the event), and they were really good.

A Little Touring

Before heading home, I spent one more day in Amsterdam, where I visited the Keukenhof Gardens and tried the famous Dutch fries on the streets of Amsterdam.

Final Thoughts

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 felt like more than just a conference; it felt like a community. People weren’t just there to present; they were there to learn from each other, share ideas, and push things forward, especially in areas like AI and sustainability.

I left Amsterdam feeling energized and inspired. It made me appreciate how powerful open source and cloud native technologies can be, especially when people collaborate around them. And for me personally, it was a reminder that even as a student, you can contribute something meaningful to a global community leveraging open source.

】

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 是世界上最大的开源会议之一，由云原生计算基金会（Linux 基金会的一部分，负责管理 Linux 等主要项目）组织。它汇集了全球云原生社区，被选为演讲者的竞争非常激烈，接受率只有个位数。

今年 3 月，超过 13,000 人聚集在阿姆斯特丹。作为那里最年轻的演讲者之一，我有机会分享我关于使用开源项目进行飓风预测的海报。

当我第一次到达时，我真的不知所措。 RAI 会议中心很大，走进去感觉更宽敞。直到那时我才真正意识到这个事件有多大。

主题演讲室座无虚席

我的海报会议

我最初构建了我的hurri我在大三的课堂上使用计算流体动力学 (CFD) 的修改版本进行了手杖模型。随着时间的推移，它演变成一个更大的项目。

我最终结合了 Kubernetes、kgateway 和 vLLM 等工具来运行不同的服务。与 FourCastNet 和我的 CFD 模型的改进版本一起，我们的目标是使用推理感知路由使飓风预测更加高效且经济实惠。

我的海报标题为“飓风数据和未来运动预测的有效推理”。 

我的海报

我不确定是否有人会过来。但很快，人群开始形成，人数比我预期的要多。

我进行了演示，这是一次紧张但有趣的经历，然后在我的笔记本电脑上演示了我的项目并回答了一系列问题。很高兴听到人们来的原因。人们正在研究类似的天气模型，好奇高中生会说什么，甚至有一位访客来自我的家乡！

向大家展示海报向上

在我的笔记本电脑上演示我的项目

探索会话

当我不做演讲时，我会尽可能多地参加会议。

其中最酷的一个是由云原生技术驱动的高空滑翔机，展示了它飞越喜马拉雅山的镜头。看到开源技术被应用于能源和排放极低的可持续航空领域，真是太棒了。

另一个令人难忘的会议实际上是一家自行车商店和一辆自行车被带到了舞台上。他们用它来解释CNCF项目成熟度生命周期，听起来很抽象，但实际上类比起来很容易理解。

解决方案展示

我还花了很多时间参观解决方案展示。

这里有大量来自公司和开源项目的展位。在项目展馆，我与维护者和贡献者进行了交谈，这确实是最有趣的部分之一。听到人们为开源做出贡献的动机让我想自己更多地参与其中。

这次活动的规模有点疯狂：这么多公司，都想脱颖而出。许多摊位都有游戏、演示或免费赠品来吸引您的注意力。

不过，我必须限制自己（行李空间是真实存在的），所以我主要只保留必需品：一件会议 T 恤和演讲者礼物，以及一些可爱的玩具。

不断发生的一件有趣的事情是人们注意到我的徽章并说：“哦！你是演讲者？！”然后我最终会重新解释我的项目。

此外，我第一次尝试了新鲜的 stroopwafels（在活动的摊位上），它们真的很好。

一点游览

回国之前，我在阿姆斯特丹又待了一天，参观了库肯霍夫花园，并在阿姆斯特丹街头品尝了著名的荷兰薯条。

最终想法

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 感觉不仅仅是一次会议；感觉就像一个社区。人们不只是为了展示，而是为了展示。他们在那里互相学习、分享想法并推动事情向前发展，特别是在人工智能和可持续发展等领域。

离开阿姆斯特丹时，我感到充满活力和灵感。这让我意识到开源和云原生技术有多么强大，尤其是当人们围绕它们进行协作时。对我个人而言，这提醒我们，即使作为一名学生，您也可以利用开源为全球社区做出一些有意义的贡献。

item.title: 【Rethinking platform engineering through diverse perspectives at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU Amsterdam】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 阿姆斯特丹从不同角度重新思考平台工程
item.description:
【

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 in Amsterdam highlighted the evolution of cloud native practices, particularly in platform engineering, and the growing focus on inclusion and accessibility within the community. This year’s conference emphasized that technical success is inseparable from human factors: the ways engineers work together, the diversity of their experiences, and the structures that support participation.

Platform engineering beyond tools and infrastructure

Platform engineering has traditionally been framed around internal developer platforms, automation, and workflow orchestration. While these technical elements are necessary, sessions at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU stressed that the usability and effectiveness of platforms depend heavily on the perspectives of the engineers who build and maintain them.

A key session explored how platform teams can benefit from integrating varied viewpoints, featuring two CNCF Community Leaders — an OpenTelemetry contributor and Merge-Forward Lead and a CNCF Ambassador. The discussion highlighted several areas where diversity of experience influences platform design:

  • Abstraction boundaries: Decisions about what to expose to developers and what to manage internally affect usability, maintainability, and adoption.
  • Self-service workflows: Platforms that empower teams to operate independently require careful design, informed by the needs and expectations of diverse users.
  • API touchpoints and integration points: Thoughtful design of APIs can reduce friction and prevent misalignment between platform capabilities and developer workflows.
  • Onboarding paths and documentation: Clear, accessible onboarding and documentation practices increase the likelihood that platforms are adopted and maintained effectively.

My talk “Who Built This Platform” presented along Elif Samedin (AirDNA) also emphasized practical strategies for platform teams. By refining decision-making processes, optimizing feedback loops, and fostering participation within CNCF technical groups, teams can build platforms that are not only technically robust but also easier to adopt and scale. These approaches highlight a broader trend in platform engineering: success is defined not only by technical outputs but by how well platforms support their users, ultimately posing the question: who are we building platforms for in the first place?

Designing for belonging: Merge Forward panel

Complementing the technical discussion, a Merge Forward panel examined the social dimensions of platform engineering. Platform teams often encounter challenges not in YAML configurations or API design, but in attracting and retaining talent. Skilled engineers may hesitate to apply for platform roles if they do not feel they belong, creating gaps in innovation and team capacity.

Merge Forward is a CNCF initiative uniting community groups focused on disability, gender, neurodiversity, and speech diversity. The panel demonstrated how considering inclusion and belonging as core design principles can improve outcomes for platform teams. Key observations included:

  • Developer experience (DevEx) improves when teams are designed around inclusive practices.
  • Platform adoption increases when contributors feel welcome and empowered.
  • Team retention benefits from culturally supportive environments.
  • Organizational outcomes improve when human factors are incorporated into platform design, as teams become more resilient and adaptable.

Through stories from platform engineers, the panel illustrated that practices such as empathy, accessibility, and allyship are not just social ideals but practical levers that enhance team performance and platform maturity. In this context, we highlighted that resilience is built not only into systems but between people, underscoring the socio-technical nature of platform engineering.

Neurodiversity Community Hub: Making invisible experiences visible

A new addition to the Community Hub this year was the first dedicated session on neurodiversity, acknowledging the presence and contributions of neurodivergent individuals in tech while recognizing the barriers they often face. Neurodivergent engineers frequently encounter structural challenges—from hiring processes to conference networking and open source contributions—that are not addressed by technical proficiency alone.

I was deeply moved to be leading this interactive session, which provided a practical space for discussion and reflection, structured around short exercises and real-world scenarios. Attendees explored:

  • How neurodiversity shows up in tech communities.
  • Tools and practices that reduce barriers and support diverse contributors.
  • Practical techniques that participants could apply immediately in workplaces, open source projects, and community initiatives.

By creating a space for shared learning and experimentation, the session highlighted the value of recognizing and supporting diverse ways of thinking. It reinforced insights from the Merge-Forward panel: inclusive practices strengthen communities, enhance collaboration, and ultimately contribute to the sustainability of projects and platforms.

Connecting technical and human perspectives

Together, these sessions illustrate that platform engineering is intrinsically socio-technical. While internal developer platforms and automation pipelines remain central, the most sustainable and widely adopted platforms are those shaped by the experiences and perspectives of the people who interact with them.

Several patterns emerged across the talks and discussions:

  1. Participation shapes outcomes: Platforms benefit when decision-making involves diverse contributors who can identify gaps, anticipate edge cases, and optimize usability.
  2. Cultural practices affect technical success: Teams that invest in belonging, accessibility, and inclusive practices experience better adoption and retention, showing that organizational culture and platform health are deeply interconnected.
  3. Learning and reflection accelerate improvement: Both technical and human-centered sessions emphasized feedback loops—within teams, across communities, and in open source projects—as essential for refining platforms and practices.
  4. Communities extend beyond code: KubeCon EU reinforced that conferences and community initiatives are not only opportunities to share technical expertise but also to create environments that reflect and reinforce inclusive values.

By integrating these insights, platform teams can design systems that are more resilient, scalable, and usable, while contributing to a healthier, more diverse cloud-native ecosystem.

Looking forward

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 2026 demonstrated that platform engineering is evolving. Beyond technical considerations, the discipline increasingly involves understanding the human factors that shape adoption, maintainability, and long-term impact. The Merge-Forward panel and neurodiversity session provided concrete examples of how community-led initiatives and inclusive practices can inform platform engineering in practical, measurable ways.

As cloud-native communities continue to grow, the lessons from KubeCon +CloudNativeCon Amsterdam suggest that the most effective platforms will be those built not only with technical expertise but also with empathy, inclusivity, and collaboration at their core. Recognizing diverse perspectives—across technical experience, cognitive styles, and social identities—is not an optional value-add but a foundational aspect of sustainable platform engineering.

】

2026 年在阿姆斯特丹举行的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 强调了云原生实践的演变，特别是在平台工程方面，以及对社区内包容性和可访问性的日益关注。今年的会议强调，技术的成功与人的因素密不可分：工程师的合作方式、经验的多样性以及支持参与的结构。

工具和基础设施之外的平台工程

平台工程传统上是围绕内部开发人员平台、自动化和工作流程编排构建的。虽然这些技术要素是必要的，但 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 会议强调，平台的可用性和有效性在很大程度上取决于构建和维护平台的工程师的观点。

一场重要会议探讨了平台团队如何从整合不同观点中受益，其中包括两位 CNCF 社区领导者——一位 OpenTelemetry 贡献者和 Merge-Forward 负责人以及一位 CNCF 大使。讨论强调了经验多样性影响平台设计的几个领域：

  • 抽象边界：有关向开发人员公开什么以及在内部管理什么的决策会影响可用性、可维护性和采用。
  • 自助服务工作流程：支持团队独立运营的平台需要精心设计，并考虑不同用户的需求和期望。
  • API 接触点和集成点：周到的 API 设计可以减少摩擦并防止平台功能与开发人员工作流程之间出现不一致。
  • 入门路径和文档：清晰、易于理解的入门和文档实践增加了平台被有效采用和维护的可能性。

我的演讲“归属感设计：合并前向面板

补充技术讨论，合并转发小组审查了平台工程的社会维度。平台团队经常遇到的挑战不是 YAML 配置或 API 设计，而是吸引和留住人才。如果技术熟练的工程师感觉不属于自己，他们可能会犹豫是否申请平台角色，从而在创新和团队能力方面造成差距。

Merge Forward 是 CNCF 的一项倡议，旨在联合关注残疾、性别、神经多样性和言语多样性的社区团体。该小组展示了如何将包容性和归属感视为核心设计原则可以改善平台团队的成果。主要观察结果包括：

  • 当团队围绕包容性实践进行设计时，开发者体验 (DevEx) 会得到改善。
  • 当贡献者感到受欢迎并获得授权时，平台采用率就会增加。
  • 团队保留受益于文化支持性环境。
  • 当平台设计中融入人为因素时，组织成果会得到改善，团队会变得更具弹性和适应性。

通过平台工程师的故事，小组说明了同理心、可访问性和盟友关系等实践不仅是社会理想，而且是提高团队绩效和平台成熟度的实用杠杆。在这种背景下，我们强调，弹性不仅构建在系统中，而且构建在人与人之间，强调了平台工程的社会技术性质。

神经多样性社区中心：让无形的体验变得可见

今年社区中心新增了首次专题会议神经多样性，承认神经多样性个体在科技领域的存在和贡献，同时认识到他们经常面临的障碍。神经发散的工程师经常遇到结构性挑战——从招聘流程到会议网络和开源贡献——这些挑战仅靠技术熟练程度是无法解决的。

能够主持这次互动会议，我深受感动，它提供了一个讨论和反思的实用空间，围绕简短的练习和现实场景进行组织。与会者探讨：

  • 神经多样性如何在科技社区中体现。
  • 可减少障碍并支持多元化贡献者的工具和实践。
  • 参与者可以立即在工作场所、开源项目和社区计划中应用的实用技术。

item.title: 【CNCF and PyTorch communities come together at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon China 2026: CFP now open】CNCF 和 PyTorch 社区齐聚 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon China 2026：CFP 现已开放
item.description:
【

The cloud native, open infrastructure, and AI communities are coming together this year at one of Asia’s flagship open source events.

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon + OpenInfra Summit Asia + PyTorch Conference China 2026 will take place from September 8–9, 2026 in Shanghai, bringing multiple open source ecosystems onto a single stage.

The event brings together communities including the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), OpenInfra, and the PyTorch Foundation.

One stage for cloud native, OpenInfra, and AI

This year’s event features the addition of PyTorch Conference China, co-located with KubeCon + CloudNativeCon and OpenInfra Summit Asia.

The event provides a space for developers, architects, maintainers, researchers, and enterprise leaders to exchange ideas, share production experience, and explore open source technologies across cloud native infrastructure and AI systems.

Call for Proposals (CFP) now open

The Call for Proposals (CFP) is now open, inviting submissions for:

  • Presentations
  • Panel discussions
  • Tutorials
  • Lightning talks

Proposals are expected to address real-world challenges and encourage in-depth technical discussion.

Suggested topics for proposals include:

  • AI / Machine Learning / Agentic AI / Data Systems
  • AI Infrastructure / Accelerators / Performance Engineering
  • Platform Engineering / Cloud Native Architecture
  • Application Development / Developer Experience
  • Operations / Observability / Reliability
  • Security / Privacy / Confidential Computing
  • Networking / Edge Computing / Distributed Systems
  • Cloud Infrastructure / Virtualization / Storage
  • Emerging Technologies / Academic Research / Advanced Topics
  • Open Source Community / Onboarding / Collaboration
  • Hardware Enablement / Diversity

You may submit your Call For Proposals here. CFP Deadline: May 3, 2026.

Registration details

Registration is now open, with three pass types being offered:

  • Corporate Pass – for company-sponsored attendees, including enterprise and government employees
  • Individual Pass – for self-funded participants, non-profits, or independent contributors
  • Academic Pass – for full-time students and faculty (valid institutional ID required)

Passes provide access to keynotes, breakout sessions, and event activities.

Get involved

  • Submit a proposal through the CFP
  • Register early to take advantage of discounted pricing

With multiple open source communities participating, the event offers an opportunity to engage across cloud native infrastructure and AI development in one place.

】

今年，云原生、开放基础设施和人工智能社区将齐聚亚洲旗舰开源活动之一。

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon + OpenInfra 亚洲峰会 + PyTorch 中国 2026 大会将于2026 年 9 月 8 日至 9 日在上海举行，将多个开源生态系统汇聚到一个舞台上。

该活动汇集了云原生计算基金会 (CNCF)、OpenInfra 和 PyTorch 基金会等社区。

云原生、OpenInfra 和 AI 的一个阶段

今年的活动新增了PyTorch 中国会议，与 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 和 OpenInfra 亚洲峰会同期举行。

该活动为开发人员、架构师、维护人员、研究人员和企业领导者提供了一个交流想法、分享生产经验并探索跨云原生基础设施和人工智能系统的开源技术的空间。

提案征集 (CFP) 现已开放

提案征集 (CFP) 现已开放，邀请提交以下内容：

  • 演示文稿
  • 小组讨论
  • 教程
  • 闪电演讲

提案预计将解决现实世界的挑战并鼓励深入的技术讨论。

建议的提案主题包括：

  • 人工智能/机器学习/代理人工智能/数据系统
  • 人工智能基础设施/加速器/性能工程
  • 平台工程/云原生架构
  • 应用开发/开发者经验
  • 运营/可观察性/可靠性
  • 安全/隐私/机密计算
  • 网络/边缘计算/分布式系统
  • 云基础设施/虚拟化/存储
  • 新兴技术/学术研究/高级主题
  • 开源社区/入门/协作
  • 硬件支持/多样性

您可以在此提交您的提案征集。 CFP 截止日期： 2026 年 5 月 3 日。

注册详细信息

注册现已开放，提供三种通行证类型：

  • 企业通行证 - 适用于公司赞助的与会者，包括企业和政府雇员
  • 个人通行证 - 适用于自筹资金的参与者、非营利组织或独立贡献者
  • 学术通行证 - 适用于全日制学生和教师（需要有效的机构 ID）

通过通行证可以访问主题演讲、分组会议和活动。

参与其中

  • 通过 CFP 提交提案
  • 尽早注册即可享受折扣价

有多个开源社区参与，该活动提供了一个在一个地方参与云原生基础设施和人工智能开发的机会。

item.title: 【Peer-to-Peer acceleration for AI model distribution with Dragonfly】使用 Dragonfly 进行 AI 模型分发的点对点加速
item.description:
【

The problem: AI model distribution is broken at scale

Large-scale AI model distribution presents challenges in performance, efficiency, and cost.

Consider a typical scenario: an ML platform team manages a Kubernetes cluster with 200 GPU nodes. A new version of a 70B parameter model becomes available — for example, DeepSeek-V3 at approximately 130 GB. Each node requires a local copy, resulting in 26 TB of data transferred from a single model hub, often through shared origin infrastructure, network bandwidth, and rate limits.

The scale of modern model hubs highlights these challenges:

  • Hugging Face Hub serves over 1 million models, with individual files regularly exceeding 10 GB (safetensors, GGUF quantizations).
  • ModelScope Hub hosts over 10,000 models — including large models such as Qwen, Yi, and inclusionAI’s Ling series — supporting a rapidly growing global user base.

These platforms have significantly improved access to open models, but distributing large artifacts across many nodes introduces system-level constraints:

  • Git LFS, which underpins large file storage on these platforms, is optimized for versioning and access rather than large-scale fan-out distribution.
  • Rate limits can affect both unauthenticated and authenticated requests under burst traffic.
  • Network costs increase as the same data is transferred repeatedly across environments.

Existing approaches — such as NFS mounts, pre-built container images, or object storage mirrors — can help mitigate these issues, but may introduce operational complexity, stale-model risk, or additional storage overhead.

This raises an important question: how can infrastructure enable model distribution to scale efficiently, so that downloading to the 200th node is as fast as downloading to the first, regardless of the model hub?

That’s exactly what the new hf:// and modelscope:// protocol support in Dragonfly delivers.

What Is Dragonfly?

Dragonfly is a CNCF Graduated project that provides a P2P-based file distribution system. Originally built for container image distribution at Alibaba-scale (processing billions of requests daily), Dragonfly turns every downloading node into a seed for its peers.

Core Architecture:

Figure 1: End-to-end flow of the P2P model distribution in Dragonfly. The Seed Peer fetches the model from the origin hub once (Step 1), the Dragonfly Scheduler computes the P2P topology (Step 3), and GPU nodes share pieces via micro-task distribution (Step 5) — reducing origin traffic from 26 TB to ~130 GB across a 200-node cluster.

The magic: Dragonfly splits files into small pieces and distributes them across the P2P mesh. The origin (Hugging Face Hub or ModelScope Hub) is hit once by the seed peer. Critically, the Seed Peer does not need to finish downloading the entire model before sharing with other peers — as soon as any single piece is downloaded, it can be shared immediately. This piece-based streaming download means distribution begins in parallel with the initial fetch, dramatically reducing total transfer time. For a 130 GB model across 200 nodes, origin traffic drops from 26 TB to ~130 GB — a 99.5% reduction.

Until now, Dragonfly supported HTTP/HTTPS, S3, GCS, Azure Blob Storage, Alibaba OSS, Huawei OBS, Tencent COS, and HDFS backends. But the two largest sources of AI model artifacts — Hugging Face and ModelScope — required users to pre-resolve hub URLs into raw HTTPS links, losing authentication context, revision pinning, and repository structure awareness.

Not anymore.

Introducing native model hub protocols in Dragonfly

With two new backends merged into the Dragonfly client, dfget (Dragonfly’s download tool) now natively understands both Hugging Face and ModelScope URLs. No proxies. No URL rewriting. No wrapper scripts.

The hf:// Protocol — Hugging Face hub

Merged via PR #1665, this backend adds first-class support for downloading from the world’s largest open-source model repository.

URL format:

hf://[<repository_type>/]<owner>/<repository>[/<path>]


Components:

Usage examples:

# Download a single model file with P2P acceleration
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/model.safetensors \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/model.safetensors

# Download an entire repository recursively
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1 \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/ -r

# Download a specific dataset
dfget hf://datasets/huggingface/squad/train.json \
  -O /data/squad/train.json

# Access private repositories with authentication
dfget hf://owner/private-model/weights.bin \
  -O /models/private/weights.bin \
  --hf-token=hf_xxxxxxxxxxxxx

# Pin to a specific model version
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/model.safetensors --hf-revision v2.0 \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/model.safetensors

The modelscope:// Protocol — ModelScope hub

Merged via PR #1673, this backend brings the same P2P-accelerated experience to ModelScope Hub — Alibaba’s open model platform hosting thousands of models, with particularly strong coverage of Chinese-origin LLMs and multimodal models.

URL Format:

modelscope://[<repo_type>/]<owner>/<repo>[/<path>]

Components:

Usage examples

# Download a model repository with P2P acceleration
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1 \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/ -r

# Download a single file
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/config.json \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/config.json

# Download with authentication for private repos
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/config.json \
  -O /tmp/config.json --ms-token=<token>

# Download a dataset
dfget modelscope://datasets/damo/squad-zh/train.json \
  -O /data/squad-zh/train.json

# Download from a specific revision
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/config.json --ms-revision v2.0 \
  -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/config.json

Under the hood: Technical deep dive

Both implementations live in the Dragonfly Rust client as new backend modules. Here’s how they work at the systems level.

1. Pluggable Backend Architecture

Dragonfly uses a pluggable backend system. Each URL scheme (http, s3, gs, hf, modelscope, etc.) maps to a backend that implements the Backend trait:

#[tonic::async_trait]
pub trait Backend {
    fn scheme(&self) -> String;
    async fn stat(&self, request: StatRequest) -> Result<StatResponse>;
    async fn get(&self, request: GetRequest) -> Result<GetResponse<Body>>;
    async fn put(&self, request: PutRequest) -> Result<PutResponse>;
    async fn exists(&self, request: ExistsRequest) -> Result<bool>;
}

Both hf and modelscope backends are registered as builtin backends in the BackendFactory, sitting alongside HTTP, object storage, and HDFS:

// Hugging Face backend
self.backends.insert(
    "hf".to_string(),
    Box::new(hugging_face::HuggingFace::new(self.config.clone())?),
);

// ModelScope backend
self.backends.insert(
    "modelscope".to_string(),
    Box::new(modelscope::ModelScope::new()?),
);

This means both schemes are available everywhere dfget or the Dragonfly daemon operates — no additional configuration needed.

2. URL parsing: Same grammar, different conventions

Both backends share the same URL grammar — scheme://[type/]owner/repo[/path] — but respect each platform’s conventions:

3. Two download modes (both backends)

Single file mode (e.g., hf://owner/repo/file.bin or modelscope://owner/repo/file.bin): 

  1. Parse URL → extract file path 
  2. Build platform-specific download URL 
  3. stat() performs a HEAD request to get content length and validate existence 
  4. get() streams the file content through Dragonfly’s piece-based P2P network 
  5. For HF: Git LFS redirects are handled transparently by the HTTP client

Repository mode (e.g., hf://owner/repo -r or modelscope://owner/repo -r): 

  1. Parse URL → no file path present 
  2. Call platform-specific API to list repository files 
  3. Deserialize the repository metadata into a file listing 
  4. For each file, construct a scheme-native URL (not raw HTTPS), preserving backend semantics 
  5. Dragonfly’s recursive download engine processes each file through the P2P mesh

This is a crucial design decision: recursive downloads emit hf:// or modelscope:// URLs back into the download pipeline, not raw HTTPS URLs. This preserves authentication context and ensures every file in the recursive download goes through the correct backend — maintaining token forwarding and URL semantics.

4. Platform-specific API integration

Hugging Face uses a resolve-based download pattern where the server may return the file directly or redirect to Git LFS storage for large model files. The reqwest HTTP client follows these redirects automatically, making LFS handling completely transparent.

ModelScope uses a structured REST API with explicit endpoints for file listing (/repo/files). The API returns a JSON envelope with Code, Data, and Message fields. The file listing endpoint supports recursive traversal natively via the Recursive=true parameter, returning structured RepoFile objects with name, path, type, and size metadata.

5. Authentication

Both backends support token-based authentication via CLI flags and bearer token headers:

# Hugging Face authentication
dfget hf://owner/private-model/weights.bin \
  --hf-token=hf_xxxxxxxxxxxxx

# ModelScope authentication
dfget modelscope://owner/private-model/config.json \
  --ms-token=<token>

Tokens propagate through all operations (stat, get, exists), enabling access to private repositories and gated models on both platforms.

Real-world impact: Where this matters

1. Multi-node GPU cluster model deployment

In large-scale enterprise environments — the kind I architect and operate daily — distributing a 130 GB model like meta-llama/Llama-2-70b across 50 GPU nodes creates a debilitating network bottleneck. I’ve seen this pattern cripple deployment velocity firsthand.

Before: Each of your 50 GPU nodes downloads the model independently. 

  • Total bandwidth: 6.5 TB from the model hub 
  • Time: Limited by origin server throughput and rate limits 
  • Cost: Full internet egress x 50

After: Seed peer fetches once, P2P distributes across the cluster. 

  • Origin bandwidth: ~130 GB (once) 
  • Time: Near-wire-speed from local peers after initial seed 
  • Cost: Minimal egress, heavy intra-cluster traffic (free)

When you’re managing self-healing, multi-cloud Kubernetes clusters at enterprise scale, this kind of origin traffic reduction isn’t an optimization — it’s a prerequisite for operational sanity.

2. Multi-hub model sourcing

Teams increasingly source models from multiple hubs. A team might use Llama from Hugging Face and Qwen from ModelScope. With both backends built in, Dragonfly becomes the unified distribution layer regardless of origin:

# From Hugging Face
dfget hf://meta-llama/Llama-2-7b -O /models/llama2/ -r

# From ModelScope
dfget modelscope://qwen/Qwen-7B -O /models/qwen/ -r

Same P2P mesh. Same caching layer. Same operational model. Different origins.

3. CI/CD for ML pipelines

Model evaluation pipelines that spin up ephemeral runners to test against specific model versions benefit from revision pinning: HTTP client follows these redirects automatically:

# Deterministic model versions in CI — from either hub
dfget hf://org/model --hf-revision abc123def -O /workspace/model/ -r
dfget modelscope://org/model --ms-revision v1.0 -O /workspace/model/ -r

Combined with Dragonfly’s caching layer, repeated CI runs across different runners pull from local P2P cache instead of remote hubs. In the enterprise CI/CD systems I’ve built, this eliminates one of the last remaining sources of non-deterministic pipeline failures: flaky model downloads.

4. Cross-platform model sourcing

For organizations utilizing global infrastructure, Hugging Face serves as the primary hub. Dragonfly’s dual-hub support enables a single distribution platform that routes to the optimal origin:

# Global clusters pull from Hugging Face
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1 -O /models/DeepSeek-R1/ -r

5. Air-gapped and edge deployments

For environments with limited or no internet access — common in regulated enterprise and financial services infrastructure — Dragonfly’s seed peer can be pre-loaded from an internet-connected staging area. Once seeded, internal nodes use P2P to distribute models without any external connectivity.

6. Dataset distribution for training

Large-scale training jobs often need the same dataset replicated across data-parallel workers:

# From Hugging Face
dfget hf://datasets/allenai/c4/en/train-00000-of-01024.json.gz \
  -O /data/c4/train-00000.json.gz

# From ModelScope
dfget modelscope://datasets/damo/squad-zh/train.json \
  -O /data/squad-zh/train.json

P2P distribution turns O(N) origin fetches into O(1) origin + O(log N) P2P propagation.

Comparison: Why not just use platform CLIs?

Platform-specific CLIs are excellent for individual developer workflows. The native protocol support in Dragonfly is for infrastructure-scale model distribution.

Getting started

Prerequisites

  • Dragonfly cluster deployed (scheduler + seed peer + peer on nodes)
  • dfget CLI available on target machines

Quick Start

1. Install Dragonfly (via Helm for Kubernetes):

helm repo add dragonfly https://dragonflyoss.github.io/helm-charts/
helm install dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly \
  --namespace dragonfly-system --create-namespace

2. Download models with P2P from either hub:

# From Hugging Face
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/model.safetensors -O ./model.safetensors

# From ModelScope
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1/config.json -O ./config.json

# Recursive repository download (works with both)
dfget hf://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1 -O ./DeepSeek-R1/ -r --hf-token=$HF_TOKEN
dfget modelscope://deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1 -O ./DeepSeek-R1/ -r --ms-token=$MS_TOKEN

3. Verify P2P is working:

# Check Dragonfly daemon logs for peer transfer activity
journalctl -u dfdaemon | grep "peer task"

What’s next

These two backends are just the beginning. The architecture is designed for extensibility — adding support for additional model hubs follows the same pattern: implement the Backend trait, register the scheme, and the entire P2P mesh instantly serves the new source. Potential future enhancements include:

  • Intelligent pre-warming: Automatically seed popular models across clusters based on usage patterns.
  • Deduplication across revisions: Share common pieces between model versions (e.g., shared tokenizer files).
  • Cross-hub deduplication: When the same model exists on both Hugging Face and ModelScope, share pieces across download sources.
  • Integration with Kubernetes model serving frameworks: Native support in KServe, Triton Inference Server, and vLLM for P2P model loading.
  • Bandwidth-aware scheduling: Prioritize P2P transfers based on GPU node topology and network proximity.

Contributing

The PRs that brought these features to life: – 

  • dragonflyoss/client#1665 — Rust implementation of the hf:// backend (Hugging Face) 
  • dragonflyoss/client#1673 — Rust implementation of the modelscope:// backend (ModelScope) 
  • dragonflyoss/d7y.io#386 — Documentation for the hf:// protocol in dfget
  • dragonflyoss/d7y.io#396 — Documentation for the modelscope:// protocol in dfget

Dragonfly is a CNCF Graduated project and welcomes contributions. If you’re working on AI infrastructure and have ideas for improving model distribution, check out the Dragonfly GitHub repository and join the community.

Conclusion

The AI industry’s model distribution problem doesn’t need another wrapper script or another S3 bucket. It needs infrastructure-level P2P distribution with first-class understanding of where models live — whether that’s Hugging Face, ModelScope, or the next model hub that emerges.

Dragonfly now speaks both hf:// and modelscope:// natively: authenticated, revision-aware, P2P-accelerated paths from the world’s two largest model hubs to every node in your cluster. One origin fetch per hub. Peer-distributed propagation. No operational overhead.

The models are getting bigger. The clusters are getting larger. The hubs are multiplying. The distribution layer needs to keep up.

Now it can.

Pavan Madduri is a Senior Cloud Platform Engineer at W.W. Grainger and a CNCF Golden Kubestronaut. He specializes in architecting massive, self-healing multi-cloud infrastructure and pioneering ‘Agentic Ops’ for enterprise Kubernetes environments. He is an active contributor to the cloud-native ecosystem, focusing on observability and high-performance container distribution. Follow his work on GitHub.

问题：AI 模型分布大规模被破坏

大规模人工智能模型分发带来了性能、效率和成本方面的挑战。

考虑一个典型场景：ML 平台团队管理一个具有 200 个 GPU 节点的 Kubernetes 集群。新版本的 70B 参数模型现已推出，例如大约 130 GB 的 DeepSeek-V3。每个节点都需要一个本地副本，从而通常通过共享源基础设施、网络带宽和速率限制从单个模型中心传输 26 TB 的数据。

现代模型中心的规模凸显了这些挑战：

  • Hugging Face Hub 为超过 100 万个模型提供服务，单个文件通常超过 10 GB（安全张量、GGUF 量化）。
  • ModelScope Hub 拥有 10,000 多个模型，包括 Qwen、Yi 和包容人工智能的 Ling 系列等大型模型，为快速增长的全球用户群提供支持。

这些平台显着改善了对开放模型的访问，但在许多节点上分布大型工件会引入系统级限制：

  • Git LFS 是这些平台上大型文件存储的基础，它针对版本控制和访问进行了优化，而不是大规模扇出分发。
  • 速率限制可能会影响突发流量下未经身份验证和经过身份验证的请求。
  • 随着相同的数据在环境中重复传输，网络成本会增加。

现有方法（例如 NFS 安装、预构建容器映像或对象存储镜像）可以帮助缓解这些问题，但可能会带来操作复杂性、过时模型风险或额外的存储开销。

这提出了一个重要问题：基础设施如何使模型分发能够有效扩展，以便无论模型中心如何，下载到第 200 个节点的速度与下载到第一个节点的速度一样快？

这正是 Dragonfly 中新的 hf:// 和 modelscope:// 协议支持所提供的功能。

什么是蜻蜓？

Dragonfly是一个CNCF毕业项目，提供基于P2P的文件分发系统。 Dragonfly 最初是为阿里巴巴规模的容器镜像分发（每天处理数十亿个请求）而构建的，它将每个下载节点都变成了其对等节点的种子。

核心架构：
